Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Welcome to The Makepeace Business System In Action with Parris Lampropoulos
We’ve covered a lot of ground in the past seven modules.
First, Clayton revealed his incredibly successful business model – and how you can adapt
it to your own business.
We also learned how to make a big splash … how to use the Makepeace Money Multiplier
to hone your knowledge of marketing concepts … and how to pitch the deal so that any
client or copywriter would practically sell their mother’s soul to partner up with you.
We covered not one, not two, but three question and answer sessions …
And we’re still not done yet.
Because frankly, Clayton won’t be satisfied until he’s proven beyond the shadow of a
doubt that his system can not only work for you, but bring in boatloads of money for
everyone involved.
So to help prove his point, he’s called in one of the hottest copywriters in the business.
We’re talking none other than Paris Lampropolous!
Over the years, Paris has written control package after control package. Like Clayton, he’s
made his clients – and himself – a ton of money.
And while he started out as a freelance copywriter, one of the main reasons he’s
become so successful is that he was able to expand his role and establish himself as an
indispensible part of his clients’ businesses.
Now, Clayton has gotten Paris to talk about how he was able to make the transition – and
how you can, too. He’ll share with you how to get a client to see you as more than “just a
copywriter” … key areas to focus on that can leverage into quick profits for both you and
your client … and much, much more.
Remember – you’ll find the transcript on your Bonus CD. Feel free to print it out and follow
along!
Now, let’s join Clayton and Paris …
Clayton: 	Hi, Parris.
Parris: 	Hey Clayton, how are you?

-- 1 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 2 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Clayton: 	I’m good. How are you?
Parris: 	Good, thanks.
Clayton: 	Good. I appreciate you doing this.
Parris: 	Hey, I love doing this.
Clayton: 	Oh cool. Okay, well I appreciate it.
So, how long had you been freelancing before you did your partnering deal?
Parris: 	I’m thinking it’s about 10 years. I had written a lot of packages for a lot of
people. I had gotten a lot of controls under my belt. I had a very strong
batting average, as far as how many packages you write end up becoming a
control. It’s always hovered in the high 80’s. The benefit of that is clients tend
to listen to what you say.
One day I called a client of mine who I had written a bunch of packages for.
Everything I had ever written for them was a control, sometimes against stiff
competition.
I said to them, “Hey, I’m going to be in your town next month,” which was
true. “What if I stop by and we try something a little something new.”
So I stopped by there and I spent the day with them. The first thing we
did was we pulled out a bunch of inserts that they were running in their
newsletters, where they advertise different products and it rides along with
the newsletter. Just on the spot I was like changing their headlines. It was a
very easy segue because it was still copy related, you know?
Then from there we did something that was not so much copy. It’s sort of like
expanding the person’s comfort zone gradually.
So if you have people listening to this call who say, “I’m a copywriter, but the
client doesn’t view me as a marketing expert or a management expert. They
view me as a copywriter.”
My answer to that is first of all this is assuming you do know your stuff. Step
one is actually to learn the parts of the business.
But assuming you know that – and we can talk about that more if you want
– the two elements I think are number one risk reversal. “I’m going to be in

-- 2 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
your town. Why don’t I stop by and we’ll try a few things.” There’s really no
risk for the client to agree.
Number two is you sort of gradually expand their comfort zone.
Are you a baseball fan, Clayton?
Clayton: 	I’d say on a scale of one to ten, I’m maybe a three.
Parris: 	Oh good, I picked the perfect analogy! [laughing]
Yeah, hopefully some people who are listening to this know baseball.
There’s this whole idea about the pitcher nipping at the corners of the strike
zone and as the game goes on the strike zone expands and the umpire is
calling strikes where a pitch that may have been called a ball early on is
called a strike. He’s just gradually expanding the strike zone of the umpire.
It’s the same thing with your client’s comfort zone. I mean nowadays I’m
helping clients with everything from hiring decisions, recruiting people, all
kinds of stuff that really on the face of it I would have no business talking
with them about.
It started out with copy. From copy it’s a natural segue into other stuff like,
“Have you ever tested this mailing list? Have you ever done this format?
What’s your lifetime value of your customer?” and things like that.
Then the more success you have with them, the more willing they are to
listen to other things.
There’s this funny concept that when somebody becomes a fan of yours, for
lack of a better word, they want everything you’ve got, right?
So if you look at Dr. Phil, he did a book on weight loss. Well, look at the guy.
This is not a guy who should be giving you weight loss advice, right? [Both
laugh] People wanted whatever they could get from Dr. Phil.
So you look at somebody like Dan Kennedy who started with marketing
stuff. Then he started selling time management stuff. Then he started selling
all kinds of other business related stuff to his customers because they
wanted it.
I think the same principle applies here.

-- 3 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Clayton: 	Sure, and what you’re doing is building credibility on credibility. I have a
couple of observations.
First of all, you went in and you showed that you knew what you were doing.
You established credibility with copywriting and you gave your clients some
winners.
It’s almost like boiling a frog, you know? So when you suggested a headline
change then, it was only natural that they would want to test that because
you had proven yourself, right?
Then as you get more deeply involved with the company you see other
opportunities. Like you said, everything from staffing, hiring, organization,
marketing ideas, strategies, media and other things that just kind of comes
naturally.
The two observations there are it seems to work best when it’s not so much
a contrived sell but kind of a natural process of expanding your contribution
to the client.
Parris: 	I think that’s dead on, Clayton. It’s a natural process. I think the second thing
we did actually was phone scripts. “What’s your upsell? What’s your cross-
sell? What are you saying to people who call in?” That’s still copywriting,
right?
So then from there it went on to other stuff. Like you said, it’s a natural
progression. There’s nothing forced about it. You’re not like pushing yourself
on them. You’re just giving them what they want and need.
Clayton: 	You’re not walking in the door and saying, “I’m a marketing consultant” or
“I’m a business growth specialist.”
Parris: 	Right. You know what the definition of a consultant is.
Clayton: 	What’s that?
Parris: 	Somebody who know 100 different sex positions but can’t get a date on a
Saturday night.
Clayton: 	[laughing] That’s great! Great on the advice, lousy on the execution.
Parris: 	Right. Well, I’m even lousy on execution with this stuff. They have to be
executing it for the most part.

-- 4 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Copy, certainly I would execute and all that, but there’s a lot of things that a
consultant will not execute for you.
So if someone listening to this is a client, they’ve got to understand that.
However, the whole thing about consultants is that they are a dime a dozen
and a lot of them don’t know what they’re talking about. They don’t walk the
talk and have never done it and all that kind of stuff.
But again, it comes back to if you’ve proven yourself and you have a track
record, they’re going to want to try it out.
Clayton: 	Right. What advantages do you see for the client in establishing a deeper
relationship with a copywriter?
Parris: 	Well, the obvious thing is they make more money if the guy is any good,
right? The other thing is it’s easy because you’ve got somebody already
there. You don’t have to go out and look for someone and try to sort out
who’s good, who’s not good, who knows what they’re talking about, who
doesn’t. Who has your best interest at heart versus who’s just in it for
themselves.
Clayton: 	Yeah, and getting on the schedule of somebody like you is not the easiest
thing in the world. I know that from personal experience. Good copywriters
tend to be busy and when the client wants you there’s a good chance you’re
not going to be available.
Who raises the issue of kind of formalizing this deeper relationship first?
Was that an idea you took to the client?
Parris: 	I think so. I don’t remember exactly how it went down. But I would assume I
did.
When you’re a consultant and you’re giving somebody ideas, if there’s no
understanding between you and the client, whether it’s formalized in writing
or it’s a handshake or whatever, then there’s no residual income for you as
the consultant.
Then you’re just trading hours for dollars, which is okay, but the best way to
do it is on performance. If you really are delivering the results then they’re
going to be happy to do that because it’s a win for them and it’s a win for
you. It keeps you motivated to do more for them and so on and so forth.

-- 5 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Clayton: 	Were there any objections raised when the issue first came up? Now I’m
assuming that in your partnering relationship now that the agreement has
several aspects to it. Is there an exclusivity clause?
Parris: 	Put it this way, we’re living together but we never got the marriage license.
Clayton: 	[laughing] You’re living in sin.
Parris: 	We are in fact exclusive, but we never both said it’s exclusive. We both kind
of left the door open that we could reevaluate if either one of us was not
happy with the way things were going.
Clayton: 	So do you have a formalized contract of some kind or a letter of agreement
with this client?
Parris: 	Yeah, and as you said, there are different aspects to it. I should be asking
you the questions because you’ve probably taken this further than anybody
and are probably making the most out of it than anybody else.
Clayton: 	Well, I’ve written quite extensively about it. My feeling is that it will be helpful
for our Easy Writers to get another perspective on it and to see how it’s
worked out for you and also how it evolved.
I have taken it quite a ways and I think we’re getting ready to accept one
new partnering client this year. That’s going to be very interesting. We’ll get
to go through the entire process of falling in love and negotiating who sleeps
on which side of the bed and all of that fun stuff. [both laugh]
I think it’s cool to get other perspectives. How hard was it to negotiate your
agreement with the client?
Parris: 	You know I’ve got to tell you, it wasn’t hard at all. It was very easy and very
natural. There were no objections, there were no nothing. I think that comes
out of having that relationship to begin with.
You know Frank Kern, right?
Clayton: 	Sure.
Parris: 	He spoke at Rich Schefren’s big thing last month or whatever. I wasn’t there
but I got to see a video of it that Frank Kern emailed to people on his list.
He did this whole talk about getting so in tune with your market and what

-- 6 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 7 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
they want and being so in tune and understanding them so well you don’t
even have to do any selling to them.
You just say, “Hey, check this out. I’ve got this. Do you want it?” And they’re
like, “Yeah”, because what the “this” is that you’ve got so resonates with
them and so mirrors what they want that there’s no selling necessary.
Whenever you can do that, that’s a powerful thing.
Clayton: 	It’s the best kind of selling, isn’t it? No selling at all.
Parris: 	Yeah.
Clayton: 	With Weiss it was easy. I didn’t work with them for very long. I think maybe it
was five or six months. I needed to get Martin out of the office to talk to him
about some things.
I told him that we were working so well together and what we were doing
was succeeding so well that I felt we should talk about taking this thing to
the moon.
His eyes just absolutely lit up and he said, “I’ve got an idea but we can’t
talk about it in the office because there are too many interruptions and all
that kind of stuff. Why don’t we go on a cruise? We’ll take our wives. We’ll
jump on a ship for four or five days and that will give us plenty of time in the
morning while our wives are still asleep to do some brainstorming.” We both
get up at like 4:00 in the morning.
I said, “Sure.” By the time we got back from the cruise, we had a contract all
worked out and a compensation schedule and the whole nine yards.
You’re right. I think the watch word here is really “natural”. It needs to be a
natural process as you bring value to your client and he wants more of that.
Parris: 	You use the word “partner” and I think it’s an appropriate word to use.
There’s a guy named Richard Koch and he did a book called The 80/20
Principle and a Nightingale Conant audio course. It’s the Pareto Principle
which we’ve all heard of. He really goes into depth on it.
One of the things he talks about is what are the attributes of a good partner?
One of them was you just have fun together. Even if you never did any
business, you would just enjoy each other’s company.

-- 7 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
You and Martin went on a cruise. I’ve gone to family events, my family and
my client partner’s family, all kinds of fun stuff, right?
Another attribute is that you respect the person or that you both respect
each other. Without respect, it’s just not going to work out. If you think the
guy’s a dufus, it’s just not going to happen.
If you go into it saying, “I’m the marketing genius and this guy’s a dufus and
he needs me because I’m a genius and he’s a dufus,” that ain’t going to
work either.
Another one was a shared history. That goes back to what we were talking
about earlier a few minutes ago. I think there may have been one more. I
don’t remember what it was, but in any event, I highly recommend the book
and the audio course The 80/20 Principle.
Clayton: 	You know I was talking about that a little bit the other day on a webinar. It
occurred to me that partnerships are a lot like marriages in another way. The
chemistry is all important.
I don’t think enough people in the copywriting space really talk about the
importance of the chemistry and establishing a personal relationship that
allows you to enjoy working together.
Parris: 	I think it’s vitally important. I’ve had a lot of experiences where the client was
a good company and they do a lot of things right and their products are good
and all that, but there was just no chemistry. So I wrote the piece and it did
well, but it wasn’t fun, you know?
It wasn’t horrible like, “Oh my God, I hate these people.” But it wasn’t the
chemistry there. When you have chemistry, it’s like this added thing that just
makes things happen better.
Clayton: 	You know I think another one for me at least with Martin, which is interesting
– I don’t think it always has to be that you’re polar opposites, but in Martin
and my case we really are in many ways.
Martin is very left brained, very detailed oriented, always approaches every
idea from the negative side. He never falls in love with an idea. He tries to
pick it apart and identifies all of the potential problems no matter how obtuse
or how high the odds are against that problem ever occurring.
He forces me then to defend my idea and solve all of those problems for

-- 8 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
him as relating to that idea. Even when it comes to copywriting, very precise
word choice is important to him.
I on the other hand, tend to be more right brained than left brained. I tend to
fall in love with ideas very quickly and easily. I only fall out of love with them
after I’ve tried them and failed miserably. [both laugh]
So we kind of complete each other in the same way as in a good marriage
the husband and wife complete each other. Each brings something to the
party that the other one doesn’t have.
Parris: 	Yeah, it’s like when two people go into business together, you want one guy
who’s the idea guy and the other guy who’s the organizer guy. If you have
two idea guys, then nothing ever gets done. You get lots of ideas, but you
never do anything with them.
If you have two organizer guys, it’s kind of like there are no ideas.
Clayton: 	A few years ago I spent a little time with Bill Guthy and Greg Renker. As
you know they’ve been phenomenally successful. I could only guess at how
much money they’ve made, but I know just one infomercial they did pulled in
$100 million in sales. At any given time, it seems like they’ve got 40 or 50 of
them on television.
Anyway, I spent some time with them and Bill works from an office on his
property in Woodland Hills. He’s real easy going and relaxed. He’s very right
brained and very idea oriented. Greg is high energy and gets it done. He’s
execution, you know? They compliment each other wonderfully in that way.
The same thing was true with Martin and me. There is I think a lot of value in
getting a realistic perspective on what your strengths and weaknesses are in
order to better identify partners that you would work well together with.
Parris: 	Right, and by the way, Julie McManus wrote an excellent article in The Total
Package not long ago about what to look for in a client. It would be a good
idea for the listeners to go look that up on the website and read that because
it’s many of the same attributes you’d want in a partnering relationship.
Clayton: 	When you went in to your partnership with this particular client, I remember
when you were contemplating it, there didn’t seem to me to be a conscious
decision to turn it into a partnership. It was almost like you were presented
with this thing. It was almost the inevitable next stage in your relationship.

-- 9 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 0 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Parris: 	Okay, yeah. Like when you’re dating someone and it’s like, “Well, we’re
going to have to get married now or break up.”
I guess, I remember the first two people I called; actually the only two people
I called were you and Michael Masterson because you were the guys who
had done it already.
Another thing I’ve learned is that when you want to do something, talk to
people who are successful doing it. Learn from the best.
So I called you and him up and I said, “What’s customarily done? What do
you ask for? What’s the typical arrangement and all that kind of stuff?”
Clayton: 	Can we talk a little bit about the various types of compensation without
getting too specific, because there are certain confidentiality things.
Parris: 	Right, I’m not going to talk specifically about what I get or don’t get. If
someone called me the way I had called you X years ago to ask you, I
would tell them that you want some sort of base fee. You want some sort of
percentage of sales. If you can get it, equity would be nice.
Clayton: 	When you do name acquisition promotions with your client, do you still
charge in advance and a royalty?
Parris: 	Yeah, we all learned from Gary Bencivenga. I do the typical $25,000 up
front against a royalty of a nickel a piece. That works very well if you’re
working with a big mailer who mails a gazillion pieces a year, someone like
a Boardroom or a Phillips or rather Healthy Directions. I’m going to go to my
grave calling them Phillips.
Clayton: 	Yeah, me too.
Parris: 	That works very well for that type of situation. Whereas if you’re working with
a client, we mentioned Rich Schefren before, someone like that who isn’t
mailing in the mail 10 million pieces but has a high price point product with
great margins, you’re not going to charge per piece. You’re going to charge
some percentage of sales.
So that’s what we do on the front end. Then on the back end I’m getting a
percentage of back end sales separate from the acquisition things.
Clayton: 	Are you doing a lot of work on the back end? Are you writing promotions for
his customer file?

-- 10 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Parris: 	I’m not writing them so much. I do write them occasionally, but a lot of back
end stuff you don’t need me or you to write. You can get someone who just
graduated from AWAI yesterday who’s green.
If you’re doing a promotion that’s about an overstock or a scratch and dent
sale or something, you don’t need the best copywriters to write that.
If you’re doing a $5,000 per year what they used to call fax service, they
don’t call them that anymore I guess.
Clayton: 	Yeah, they’re email now.
Parris: 	Just in case people listening don’t know what that is, you’re in a financial
newsletter business and you have a newsletter that’s $200.00 per year. You
get people in the front door with that. On the back end you’re selling them
high priced email services that are more like trading stocks or options.
So the newsletter would just have like your stock portfolio and buy these,
sell these, and you get an update once a month when the newsletter comes.
The trading service would be like that morning you’ve got to do this option
or this stock or something and you’re going to make a gazillion dollars doing
these trades.
Of course you charge a lot more for that, like $2,000, $3,000 or $5,000 a
year, whatever it is.
That kind of back end promotion obviously does take a high degree of skill.
Clayton: 	Right. You want to maximize your revenue on those promotions especially
because you’re going to your customers. That turns out to be the major profit
center in most companies that do that kind of thing.
Parris: 	Right. The front end is basically a break even proposition.
Clayton: 	That’s cool and there’s an interesting point there. You’re building their
file which let’s say is basically a break-even proposition. As part of this
partnering package, you’re receiving kind of an override on back end sales
as well, even though you don’t have to write it.
Parris: 	Yes, but what I usually do is I end up critiquing it or copy-chiefing it. So
again, depending on the level of skill required, I don’t need to get involved in

-- 11 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 2 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
copy-chiefing a scratch and dent sale that says, “Hey, we’ve got these jars
of vitamins and the label has little nicks in it. It doesn’t affect the vitamin, but
is has a little nick so we can’t sell it at full price, so it’s like buy two get one
free.” Anybody can write that. It’s going to do just fine without me or you.
Whereas if it’s a back end promotion like we just talked about for a $5,000
per year trading service, well, now you need really good copy. So either I
would write it or more often another writer would write it and I’ll be the copy-
chief.
Clayton: 	That’s interesting because I wanted to talk to you about that. You get to a
point with these clients where your depth in the organization is such that you
can’t possibly write everything that needs to get written, especially as the
company grows.
I know that you’ve worked with copy cubs in the past. In fact I met some of
them and they’re wonderful people. Are all of the copywriters people that you
retain and pass the fees along to the client? Or does the client come to you
with copywriters sometimes?
Parris: 	We’ve worked it both ways. I think we’re moving more towards me just doing
it, which means I need administrative staff to handle that kind of stuff and
to keep track of everything. Then I become sort of like a virtual ad agency,
which is I guess what you are, although you’re not so virtual. You’re a real ad
agency with a building and everything.
Clayton: 	Again, that has been a very natural process.
Just a year ago Martin Weiss, my financial client, came to me and said, “You
know I love the fact that we’re really kicking it up on the back end and sales
are way up.”
It hadn’t surprised him because we had done this together before. But he
said, “I’m frustrated. I’ve got all of these other projects that I need to get
done. I need a honeymoon series for each of my seven analysts.”
Parris: 	What’s a honeymoon series?
Clayton: 	It’s when a new name is brought on the file over the internet. They’re just
leads. They are subscribing to a free ezine and usually downloading a free
report of some kind. Then over time as they bond with you they become
customers.

-- 12 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
But it costs money to produce leads now. We’re spending $300,000 a month
on pay per click. So those names come in and it’s about five or six months
before we get that $300,000 back for those 50,000 leads we generated six
months ago.
It becomes important to bring that money forward as much as possible.
Parris: 	Bring it forward meaning in the shortest amount of time? Is that what you
mean?
Clayton: 	Right. Instead of being able to break even on your pay per click in six
months, you want to break even in three months or preferably three days.
Parris: 	Right. I was just going to say, how about like a day would be cool. But see
the way it works is let’s say you were breaking even in a day because that
was your comfort level at the time.
Now you get more aggressive about getting more names that would be a
loss initially but that you’ll break even later. So now you get more aggressive
about ramping up the copy so that breaking even gets shorter time.
Then you can become more aggressive about going in the hole again to
get even more names. It’s kind of like a dog chasing its tail except in a good
way.
Clayton: 	Yeah, it’s an upward spiral.
Parris: 	An upward spiral. Thank you, I couldn’t come up with the right metaphor. It’s
an upward spiral.
Clayton: 	We started in August with $10,000 a month in pay per click. We monitored
how well the names converted. It wasn’t for six months that we got our first
$10,000 back.
But by then we were ready to move on to $50,000 then $100,000 and so
forth.
The gauntlet series or honeymoon series is simply a period of time, a week
to 10 days, at the beginning of that new lead’s lifetime in which he receives
no promotions but he gets plenty of gifts and freebies to really cement
the relationship and hopefully cause him to become dependent on the
organization.

-- 13 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Parris: 	So what would be examples of the type of freebies you are talking about?
Clayton: 	Free reports, perhaps a free live event online, a free video – anything that
brings value to his life so that he finds himself counting on you to be there
for him.
Parris: 	So what you’re talking about is the original model was it would be an e-
course, right? You’d get a five or seven installment, whatever it is and it’s
delivered as an email. In each one they learn another piece of the puzzle
then you start selling them.
But then what has happened is it’s evolved into sort of being multi media.
Clayton: 	That’s correct.
Parris: 	So there would be emails that are content. There would be downloadable
PDF’s that are content. There may be audio interviews and videos that are
content because people like to get their information different ways.
Clayton: 	Exactly.
Parris: 	The other thing is I’m wondering if this is true, we know that multi channel
customers are the best customers. In other words, if somebody bought
something from you through direct mail and then they also bought
something from you off an email or the web, that’s usually a higher spending
customer, right?
Clayton: 	That’s correct.
Parris: 	So I’m wondering if it’s the same that multi media customers are better than
single media customers.
Clayton: 	Well, you know we’re finding that they tend to be. But also customers are
not static. They evolve. So if they come into an environment where you’re
constantly changing media, they may not be comfortable sitting still and
watching a half hour or one hour video online when they first come to you.
But over time you’ll see them migrate and you’ll begin to recognize that until
last month this guy only bought from emails. He never bought as a result of
a video, but this month he bought because of a video for the first time.
We also see it in the registration rates for the online events that we do which
tend to be one hour long webinars or video events. We’ve also done a

-- 14 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
couple of teleconferences online with a client.
The first time we did it we offered the event free to the file. All they had to do
was register to say they’d be there. We got maybe 7,000 people to register.
That was about a year ago.
Now we average about 25,000 people registering. So the more we’ve
offered, the more people have come to enjoy them and set aside time for
them and use them. So that’s really cool.
You were asking about terminology. The honeymoon or gauntlet series is
nothing more than the first week or 10 days of the new name on file where
he gets a lot of freebies for the purpose of bonding him with the company.
It usually culminates in a low dollar sale of some kind.
Parris: 	You know it’s interesting that you mention that because I think it’s an
ingredient that’s missing from the way a lot of companies do business; the
bonding part of it.
It’s like, “I’ll give you great value and I will then sell you more stuff.” So I’ll
give you great value on your initial purchase or even the initial ezine and
then I’ll sell you stuff that will be great value, and a lot of people do that.
The part they miss is the bonding, creating the relationship and the feeling
that the guru is someone you know if you’re doing a guru driven thing, right?
Like if it’s a health newsletter and there’s a Dr. Whitaker or a Dr. Rowan or
Dr. Williams or Dr. Wright or whoever. How great is it if they feel like they
really know Dr. Wright?
Clayton: 	Right, and it’s more than knowing them. It’s feeling that he has their best
interest at heart and he’s their advocate.
Now, “I’m not going to let you make a mistake,” is a great Bob King line from
Phillips Financial. “My job is to make sure you don’t make a mistake and so
long as you’re with me, I’m going to make sure of that.”
There’s also the Us versus Them mentality that comes along with the
advocacy. “We both know those guys are out to get you,” whether it’s the
government or the medical profession or whatever. I’m preaching to the
choir here. Parris Lampropoulos knows this better than anybody.

-- 15 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
But establishing that Us versus Them mentality is just powerful because now
this person says, “Well, Martin is like me. Martin and I are the same kind of
guy. That means that when he tells me something I can trust him, where I
can’t trust the government or I can’t trust the doctor.”
Parris: 	Right. There’s that trust. You said something funny about that, “Martin is like
me.” That is profoundly important so I hope the listeners write that down and
burn it in their brain.
We had an incident recently where one of the editors who worked with the
guru on the newsletter asked, “what do you think the political orientation is of
the audience?” This was for a health newsletter.
I said, “Look, don’t make the mistake of making assumptions about what
their political leanings are. My guess is that their political leanings roughly
mirror the country at large. You’ve got people on the left, people on the right,
and people in the middle.”
But here’s something else I’d bet. I’d bet you if you asked them what’s Dr.
Rowan’s political leanings, they would all answer, “Oh, he’s like me.”
So all the Democrats, for lack of a better term, will say, “Oh, Dr. Rowan is
like me. He’s against those greedy drug company corporations.”
If you ask the Republicans, “Oh, Dr Rowan is like me. He’s against big
government bullies like the FDA coming and taking your supplements away.”
[laughing]
Clayton: 	That is profound. That is absolutely right. If you play your cards right, that is
exactly the effect that you’ll achieve. It’s absolutely crucial.
You know we’ve kind of gotten into marketing consulting here. This is a
seminal concept in creating a cohesive marketing plan for a company.
What have we talked about? We’ve talked about bringing people in at a
loss. We’ve talked about how you can use profits from the first month or six
weeks, three months, or six months to offset the cost of creating your lead
file.
We’ve talked about bonding with those new people. We’ve talked about
creating a sense of obligation by giving them lots of stuff for free and a
sense of dependence and a sense of loyalty.

-- 16 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 7 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
That loyalty thing is really important in another way I think. In the financial
industry for example, not all of your recommendations are going to be
winners. Sometimes the market is going to turn against you.
The fact of the matter is that the growth of your company is directly related
to how many people stay with you through those tough times.
The same is true in the health industry because you’ll go to people and say,
“This supplement’s been proven six ways from Sunday to relieve arthritis,”
but not every supplement works for everybody.
So it’s that loyalty that keeps them with you.
Parris: 	I’m going to tell you something that you already know but for the benefit for
maybe of some listeners who don’t, I know you have all levels of experience
in your customer file, people who have been around forever, longer than us,
and people who are brand new.
One thing I learned a long time ago from Howard Ruff is they buy the first
time because they’re expecting some sort of benefit. So in a financial
newsletter they’ll make money. In a health newsletter, it’ll cure some health
problem or whatever. That’s the reason they buy initially.
The reason they renew is because they like you.
Clayton: 	That’s so true.
Parris: 	So you know, the bonding stuff, presenting the information in an easy to
understand way that they don’t have to work to read the damn thing.
Clayton: 	I know one of your pet peeves is long headlines that are all upper lower
case.
Parris: 	I have a number of pet peeves about writing that isn’t clear. You know, how
dare we make people work to understand what we’re saying. These good
people gave us our money, rather gave us their money…I wonder what
Freud would say about that one.
Clayton: 	Yeah, I was about to say, that is a copywriting client in action.
Parris: 	Have you heard John Carlton say, “The universe is out of balance until that
bastard gives me his money.” [both laugh]

-- 17 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
When I’m writing the copy I’m picturing that guy sitting on his couch and he
better whip out his wallet.
Clayton: 	I tell my writers. “That prospect has your money in his pocket. Your job is to
go get it.”
Parris: 	Right, but getting back to the point I was making, these people gave us their
money. We owe it to them to give them something good and how dare we
make them work at it. They already worked. They worked the first time when
they earned the money, you know?
It’s like how do you feel if you go into some local business or restaurant or
whatever and you’re the one who has to go up to the station and get napkins
because the waiter disappeared?
Clayton: 	Or worse, I’ve had to go into the kitchen to find the waiter! [both laugh]
You’re absolutely right. The fact of the matter is they won’t work. You just
lose them.
Parris: 	Yeah, and that’s like a big loss. Heck, you’ve already got the damn
customer. That was the hard part. Now just keep the relationship and sell
them more stuff that they want. Every customer that drops off is like a huge
wasted opportunity.
Clayton: 	How much should you be willing to spend to keep a customer?
Parris: 	I think it varies by the business. You know, what’s the lifetime value and all
that stuff. Do you have a formula you use? Like a percentage?
Clayton: 	No, I think the quick easy answer is you should be willing to spend as much
to keep a customer as you spent to acquire them in the first place at a very
minimum.
Parris: 	That’s a good rule of thumb I would say.
Clayton: 	But I think your answer is actually a better one. If you dig deeper and look
at what the value of what that customer would be to you if he’s saved over a
typical lifetime, say seven years, you should be willing to spend a substantial
amount of that money.
By keeping him, you’re not just keeping him; he’s going to be telling
everybody that will listen about what a great company you are.

-- 18 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page  of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Parris: 	The one thing that people don’t understand is that’s very important and if
you’re going to be a marketing consultant you need to know this one too.
Put it this way, I’ll start it the other way around, you can do everything
right and never have a complaint and this and that and they’re going to be
pleased but they’re not going to rave about you.
Whereas, if you screw up and then make it good, now they’re going to rave
about you to everybody.
Clayton: 	Yeah, that’s absolutely true.
Parris: 	I’m not saying go screw up on purpose to use another way to fix things to
get people to rave about you. What I am saying is be on the lookout for
stuff like that. Fix them, apologize for them, take ownership of them, and
then go above and beyond fixing them. You will have the most loyal fan and
cheerleader you can imagine if you do that.
Clayton: 	That’s brilliant and it’s absolutely right. If a client comes to you and says,
“How do I increase the response of my promotions to my customer file?”
I don’t know a single copywriter that would say, “Well, first let me look at
customer service.”
What you said is exactly right. If customer service is scripted to treat
complaints like solid gold, then not only do you save those customers, but it
goes viral because they’re going to tell their friends.
Parris: 	Customer service is the big black hole in American business. It’s like so few
do it right and a lot of people think they’re doing it right and they’re always
shocked when they find out what goes on, right?
They say, “Oh yeah, we’ve got a good customer service and we do this and
that and the other thing.” Then you start monitoring it with Mystery Shopping
and stuff like that and they’re always like stunned at what’s going on.
Clayton: 	When I first went to work with Martin, we had developed a deeper
relationship and I wasn’t just freelancing, but it was very early in the
relationship. I was just sitting around with nothing to do and I thought I
wondered how his customer service handled stuff.
So I called up and I said, “Hi, I’ve got this promo here for something called

-- 19 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 20 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Safe Money Report and it looks kind of interesting. I don’t really have it right
here. What was the price for a one year subscription again?”
The woman goes, “99 bucks.”
Then there was this long silence and I said, “Oh, okay. What do I get with
that?”
She said, “There are a couple of free gifts that you get with it.” Then she
gave me a cursory description of them.
So I said, “Well, is that your best deal? Is that what I should take?”
She said, “Well, we have a two-year offer too. You save money on the thing.
You save a few bucks and you get a couple of extra gifts.”
I said, “What are the gifts?” I had to drag it out of her, right? So then after
about a 15-minute conversation I said, “So alright, you told me about the one
and two year, it sounds really good, I think I’d like three years. What do I get
with three years?”
She said, “We don’t sell three years.”
Parris: 	Was that true?
Clayton: 	No. Even if it had been true, customer service should have been a heck of a
lot sharper than that.
Parris: 	I’ll tell you something. What you just described now is typical. Let me tell you
even worse what happens. You know what I’m about to say, right? The ones
that talk you out of the sale, who say stuff like, “Well, you know what, you
should just go for the one year. The two year is no great shake; you just get
a few more reports” or whatever.
There are people out there who think capitalism is evil, that you’re taking
people’s money. There are people out there who have a scarcity mentality,
right? You don’t want those people working in your customer service.
Clayton: 	You know what? They are working in your customer service and they’re
working in your marketing department.
They’re the people who are going to be telling you, “Oh, we don’t want to
contact our customers more than once a week. We don’t want to bother

-- 20 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 2 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
them.”
When I hear that I’ll say, “Here’s somebody who should probably be a
plumber. They should not be in marketing.”
Parris: Well, if they were a plumber they’d be broke too.
Clayton: 	[laughing] But they wouldn’t be breaking me.
Parris: 	It’s like the guy who comes and does my landscaping. I shouldn’t even call it
landscaping, the guy who mows my lawn. I just want this, this, and this. Just
make everything nice. And he’s like afraid to ask me because you know he
doesn’t want to take my money.
Well, let me decide if I want to give you my money or not, you know? I don’t
want my lawn looking like crap, which it does. [laughing]
Clayton: 	But he doesn’t want to come across as a salesman.
Parris: 	Right, we have this hill, right? It’s got juniper ground cover to keep weeds
out. But that doesn’t work because weeds are coming up every time you
look.
So I’m like, “Look man, just doing this two or three times a summer aint
cutting it. I want this stuff to be good.”
He said, “Well, that’s a lot of work involved and it costs money.”
So I said, “Alright, so how much does it cost?”
Clayton: 	[laughing] Then he tried to talk you out of it.
Parris: 	Yeah, because it’s a scarcity mentality and a poverty mentality. I understand
it because I used to have it.
When I first got into mail order I started my own mail order business where I
was selling $29.00 books through the mail. Guess what? If you ain’t Rodale
or Boardroom, you can’t make money selling $29.00 books. You’ve got to
peddle like a million of them to make money.
Clayton: 	Yeah, and you didn’t have a follow-on product. I remember that story.
Parris: 	So why don’t you sell $1,000 products? Well, I can’t do that! Who’s going to

-- 21 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 22 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
buy a $1,000 product?
I just so had it in my head that who’s going to buy it? Here’s the irony,
meanwhile I was broke. I was broker than broke at the time. I was months
behind in my rent. I had no furniture. I was eating like a baked potato for
dinner, right?
And yet I would spend $1,000 to go to a Ted Nicholas seminar. I was so
broke that I couldn’t fathom that people had money to spend on stuff.
Meanwhile I was even broker than them and I was still spending money.
It’s a big mistake that people make.
Clayton: 	Yeah, it really is and that’s one thing to be on the lookout for when you’re
dealing with clients’ companies. That’s one way that you get involved in the
staffing discussions.
When you identify those weaknesses on the staff and the client says, “Why
didn’t this go out?” or, “Why did it take two months to roll out this winning
promotion?” the next thing you know you’re involved in procedures and
staffing.
I’ve replaced presidents at clients’ companies. I recruited the VP of
Marketing from that big Minnesota mail house. What is it called?
Parris: 	The one that Dan Rosenthal started?
Clayton: 	No, it’s one of the major catalogers. Anyway, I recruited their Executive VP of
Marketing and made him president of the company.
You just get involved in these things because they need to get done to
remove a road block or to eliminate a choke point in the company, some
obstacle to growth.
Again, the watchword of all of this is just that it happens naturally if you let it.
But it won’t happen unless you, in your own mind, in the copywriter’s mind,
have a different vision of where you want this to go.
All the years I did freelancing where I could have said, “Let me see your
inserts,” or “Let me see what you sell to customers on the back end,” or “Let
me see your package stuffers,” or “Let me call customer service to see if
they’re helping or hurting.”

-- 22 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 2 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
I could have done that with 10 or 15 major mailers when I was first getting
started and deepened my relationship with them, but it took me probably 10
years to.
Parris: 	You know Clayton, it’s easy to say you should have done it but maybe you
weren’t ready. There’s a learning curve that the copywriter has to go through
to know this stuff and to be able to offer it to the client. That doesn’t happen
overnight either.
I happen to be of a voracious reader and I always want to learn stuff. I think
you’re the same way. Most of the people who are really good at this game
are the same way too.
I read a lot of marketing books. I read a lot of business books. I read Peter
Drucker and all that stuff. I read all the classics like E-Myth, Good to Great,
and all that kind of stuff. Then there are books on hiring and so on and so
forth.
Of course part of the trick is separating the good books from the bad books.
You’ve got to be very careful about learning from people who walk the talk.
Like I said earlier in the call, I called you and I called Michael Masterson
because you guys walk the talk.
Clayton: 	I was talking to Pat Corpora – not the other day now, it’s been several
weeks.
Parris: 	Yeah, that was a great interview.
Clayton: 	I mean this was the guy that mailed hundreds of millions of CD-roms for
America Online and he says his biggest inspiration has been Good to Great.
So here’s a guy with a $150 million dollar marketing budget who’s basing
everything he does with a company on this book that had this profound
influence on his life.
Parris: 	Well, it’s a great book. Again, if you look at it, there’s a proof element within
the book. The guy’s not just spouting some theory. There was serious
research that went into this. It was like doing a double blind study or
something.
You want the thing to be measurable and provable and all this, but
for people getting started I think the thing you want to start with is just

-- 23 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 2 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
marketing. That’s a natural extension of copywriting.
Learn from the best. Learn from Jay Abraham. Learn from Dan Kennedy.
Learn from Joe Polish. Buy all their stuff. Devour it and internalize it until it
becomes second nature to you.
Clayton: 	I think that’s great advice.
Parris: 	Just never stop your education. I go to a lot of seminars and stuff and I buy a
lot of home study stuff like the DVD’s of the seminar or the CD’s or whatever
when I can’t make it to one.
A lot of people are sort of jaded and are like, “I’m not going to spend money
on that stuff.”
I’m like, “Hey, no problem. I’ll buy the course for $500 and then I’ll charge
you $1,000 per hour to tell you all the stuff that was in the course.” [laughing]
Clayton: 	Yeah, that’s true. If you use it and you put it to work it could be extremely
valuable.
Parris: 	I don’t think I’ve ever not made back what I spent on one of those things,
which brings me to another point. I forget where it was, but there was a
study done at maybe Harvard Business Review or one of those things, that
said one of the highest ROI activities any business could do is training.
So if you’ve got in-house copywriters, send them to Clayton’s seminar. If
you’ve got secretarial staff, teach them the shortcut things for Microsoft
Word and Excel and all that. If you’ve got customer service people, send
them to a customer service seminar.
Clayton: 	I totally agree. Boy, I’ll tell you what, it’s hard as a business owner
sometimes because I’m constantly getting requests from staff to go to these
training things.
Parris: 	It does get expensive.
Clayton: 	It’s very expensive. You want to make sure it’s not just a junket to Las
Vegas. But I’ve said no exactly once. On every other occasion I’ve sent
them. I sent four or five people to Rich’s thing.
Parris: 	Wow, that’s great.

-- 24 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 2 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Clayton: 	That’s a hearty amen to what you just said.
Parris: 	You know the big companies that you and I have done work with, Healthy
Directions and Boardroom, what they usually do is rotate.
They’ll say, “Okay, you get to go to the Gary Bencivenga 100 and you
get to go to Clayton’s thing,” and so forth. This way everybody gets to do
something. I mean, if they sent everybody at $5,000 a pop, you know.
Clayton: 	Well, Parris, listen, thanks a lot for this today. We’ve already run over by a
good 15 minutes.
Is there anything you’d like to close with?
Parris: 	Learn your stuff to be good at it and then share it with your customers and
do everything we just talked about.
I just want to close with this, that there are like four leverage points as I see
it. The first one is marketing. For the bulk of my career that was all I focused
on because that’s what I knew.
The other big leverage point is hiring. I just can’t over emphasize how much
of a difference it makes when they hire the right people to do stuff.
Another leverage point would be training.
Then there’s a fourth leverage point which is the mental mindset or
psychological mindset of the business owner.
Most ceilings to a company’s growth are not because of tangible things like,
“We couldn’t raise more money or we couldn’t rent more lists,” or that kind of
stuff.
It’s almost always mental blocks that people have about growing further and
getting outside of their comfort level. They’re afraid to be too big. It’s just
totally out of their comfort level.
So number one, make sure that your client’s comfort level is pretty big.
Number two, you could do that to yourself too. Try to always do things that
make you a little uncomfortable because that’s the only way you grow.
Clayton: 	That’s great advice. I’m going to take it. [laughing]

-- 25 of 27 --

Makepeace Business System
Module 8 • The System in Action with Parris Lampropoulos
Transcript 	Page 2 of 27
The Total Package 	www.makepeacetotalpackage.com
Parris: 	I think you’re doing pretty well in that department.
Clayton: 	It’s always good to be reminded though. As you were saying that I couldn’t
help but think about a couple of things I’m dealing with right now. You can
never think too big – getting out of that box, trying new stuff, being willing to
fail.
You know, I love the old analogy about Babe Ruth being the strikeout king.
It’s the same with business. It’s the people who never fail that really are not
producing anything.
Parris, thank you so much for this time. This is one of the best interviews
we’ve ever done.
Parris: 	Thanks. That’s quite a compliment. You’ve had a lot of terrific interviews on
here.
Clayton: 	We had lots of great content, a lot of things to think about. Thanks a lot
buddy. I appreciate it.
Parris: 	Thank you, Clayton.
Clayton: 	Okay, bye-bye.
Parris: 	Bye.
Congratulations!
You’ve just completed the eighth and final module of The Makepeace Business System.
You should be proud of yourself – because what you’ve accomplished is no small thing.
We covered a lot of material. And there’s even more on the Bonus CD in your case.
You’ll find transcripts and valuable bonus reports – plus links to MP3 recordings of each
module, so you can download them to your portable MP3 player. So be sure to check it
out.
Once you’ve done that, it’s time to put what you’ve learned into practice. And if you
implement even one-tenth of what we’ve covered, you should see your profits increase
dramatically.
Which is, of course, the reason we’re all here in the first place!

Just don’t forget to stay in touch … because Clayton wants nothing more than to brag
about how you’ve used The Makepeace Business System to catapult your business into
the stratosphere and rake in the big bucks.
So make sure you e-mail your success stories to feedback@makepeacetotalpackage.
com.
Now, get out there – and start putting The Makepeace Business System to work for you!

-- 27 of 27 --
